---
title: "Canada Toadflax (Linaria canadensis)"
slug: "linaria-canadensis"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/631130748/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Canada Toadflax"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Lamiales"
tax_family: "Plantaginaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Lamiales", "Plantaginaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/631130748/medium.jpg" alt="Canada Toadflax" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/coreytcallaghan">
coreytcallaghan</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/346249574">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Lamiales
-   **Family:** Plantaginaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=287345)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A delicate wildflower characterized by its slender stems, narrow leaves, and small bluish to purple flowers with a pale throat. In Florida, it is commonly found in dry, sandy habitats such as fields, roadsides, and open disturbed areas. It typically grows during the cooler months and often appears as a winter annual, adding subtle color to open landscapes.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/287345-Linaria-canadensis)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-linaria-canadensis/)
